skill.name: diffdock
skill.description: Use DiffDock workflows to predict protein-ligand binding poses, prepare batch docking inputs, analyze confidence scores, and document validation limits for structure-based drug design.
skill.summary: Run DiffDock molecular docking workflows for protein-ligand pose prediction and result review.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high findings, but review found the highest-severity hits are false positives from SMILES chemistry strings and scientific documentation. The real risk is medium: the skill documents shell commands, uses helper scripts, references external repositories and demos, and writes local CSV outputs, but no credential theft, exfiltration, malicious prompt injection, or destructive behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: External Command Workflows Require Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to clone external code, pull a Docker image, run Python modules, and call downstream tools such as GNINA. These commands are expected for DiffDock use, but they execute code outside the skill package and require user review of dependencies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically legitimate for a scientific docking workflow. Risk remains because they install or execute external projects and containers.

medium_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Helpers
medium_findings.description: Helper scripts read user-supplied CSV, PDB, SDF, and result directories, then write templates or summary CSV files. This is normal for docking workflows, but users should run it in a working directory where overwriting output paths is acceptable.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and observable, but they target user-selected scientific inputs and outputs. I found no evidence of hidden filesystem traversal or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Chemistry Strings Trigger Generic Secret and Crypto Rules
low_findings.description: Several static findings labeled SMILES examples and amino acid sequences as Windows SAM, C2, or weak cryptography indicators. These are false positives caused by chemical notation and biological sequence text, not credential access or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirmed these lines are ligand SMILES strings, CSV headers, or protein sequences. They do not perform credential access, cryptography, or command-and-control behavior.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Imports Are Limited to Dependency Checks
low_findings.description: The setup checker uses Python dynamic import to test whether named packages are installed. The package names are defined in local lists, and no user-controlled import target was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is real, but context shows package availability checks with fixed import names. I found no evidence that user input reaches __import__.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: A targeted search for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and similar marketplace prompt-injection patterns found no suspicious instruction text in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only match for injection-like search terms was re.IGNORECASE in a confidence-score parser. No evidence found for prompt injection or attempts to bypass audit instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell and Docker Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes bash commands for setup, inference, Docker execution, and rescoring. They are not hidden, but users should inspect commands before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible examples required for the workflow. They are potentially risky only because they execute external tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: External URLs and Model Resources
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill points users to GitHub, Hugging Face, arXiv, and installation resources. These references are expected for DiffDock, but they depend on third-party availability and trust.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: External references are explicit and relevant. I found no evidence that the skill silently sends user data to those URLs.

content.value_statement: Protein-ligand docking workflows can be hard to configure and interpret. This skill guides DiffDock setup, batch inputs, confidence review, and validation planning for research use.
actual_capabilities: Guides single protein-ligand docking with PDB files, protein sequences, SMILES strings, SDF files, and MOL2 files.
Helps create and validate batch CSV inputs for multiple protein-ligand complexes.
Explains DiffDock confidence scores and separates pose confidence from binding affinity.
Provides workflows for virtual screening, ensemble docking, rescoring, and energy minimization.
Checks local DiffDock dependencies, CUDA availability, RDKit, ESM, and related packages.
Summarizes, ranks, filters, and exports DiffDock prediction results.
limitations: It does not predict binding affinity, ADMET properties, kinetics, or selectivity.
It requires a working DiffDock installation, and GPU hardware is strongly recommended.
Predictions require scientific validation before research or development decisions.
Large biomolecules, covalent docking, membrane proteins, and unusual ligands may need other tools.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Single Docking Run
use_cases.description: Set up a protein and ligand input, choose DiffDock defaults, and understand the expected output files.

use_cases.title: Screen a Ligand Library
use_cases.description: Build a batch CSV, precompute embeddings, run virtual screening, and extract top-confidence poses.

use_cases.title: Review Docking Results
use_cases.description: Classify confidence scores, identify low-quality predictions, and plan rescoring or experimental validation.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Docking Run
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run DiffDock for one protein PDB file and one ligand SMILES string. Explain the inputs, command, outputs, and confidence score meaning.

prompt_templates.title: Create Batch Inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DiffDock batch plan for these proteins and ligands. Include the CSV columns, validation checks, and output organization.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Difficult Predictions
prompt_templates.prompt: My DiffDock predictions have low confidence for a flexible ligand. Recommend parameter changes, sampling settings, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Screening Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a high-throughput DiffDock screening workflow with precomputed embeddings, result ranking, rescoring, and experimental follow-up criteria.

output_examples.input: User asks how to dock aspirin against a prepared protein structure.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a concise run plan with required files, the DiffDock inference command, expected SDF outputs, and confidence interpretation notes.

output_examples.input: User provides a ligand library and one target protein for virtual screening.
output_examples.output: The skill describes a batch CSV layout, validation steps, embedding reuse, GPU settings, and a ranking workflow for top predicted poses.

output_examples.input: User asks whether a high confidence score means strong binding.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that confidence measures pose certainty, not affinity, and recommends rescoring plus experimental validation.

best_practices: Validate protein structures, ligand formats, and batch CSV files before running long jobs.
Use multiple samples and inspect top poses before trusting a single prediction.
Combine DiffDock confidence with rescoring, refinement, and experimental evidence.
anti_patterns: Treating confidence scores as binding affinity or potency measurements.
Running large screening jobs on CPU without testing a small case first.
Using DiffDock for covalent docking, protein-protein docking, or unvalidated clinical decisions.

faq.question: Does this skill run DiffDock by itself?
faq.answer: It guides commands and helper scripts. You still need a working local or container DiffDock installation.

faq.question: Can it predict binding affinity?
faq.answer: No. It predicts binding poses and confidence scores. Use rescoring or free energy tools for affinity estimates.

faq.question: What inputs does it support?
faq.answer: It covers protein PDB files, protein sequences, ligand SMILES strings, and ligand structure files such as SDF or MOL2.

faq.question: Is a GPU required?
faq.answer: A GPU is not always required, but it is strongly recommended for practical runtimes and batch screening.

faq.question: Can it handle virtual screening?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides CSV preparation, batch execution guidance, embedding reuse, and result ranking workflows.

faq.question: How should I validate results?
faq.answer: Inspect poses visually, compare interactions, use complementary scoring, and confirm important findings experimentally.
